what_i_noticed: 9 API routers with zero test coverage. No validation of HTTP status codes, query parameter bounds, Pydantic model enforcement, or input sanitization at the HTTP layer. The backtest router had a hardcoded TTL (86400) instead of using config, and accepted arbitrary strings as date parameters with no format validation — SQL injection payloads would pass through to pandas. Phase A Finding #1 (macro_risk zero weight) was a FALSE POSITIVE: config.py already had macro_risk: 0.12 in signal_weights; I was reading the fallback dict in signal_engine.py which is never used at runtime.
hypothesis: Adding comprehensive router-level tests with hardened input validation on the backtest endpoint closes the largest coverage gap and prevents injection/crash vectors at the API boundary.
what_i_did: 1. Created test_routers.py with 144 tests across 14 test classes covering 8 of 9 routers (all except sector, which has no user input to validate). Tests cover: HTTP method enforcement (405), query parameter bounds, Pydantic model validation for all POST endpoints, ticker sanitization (SQL injection, XSS, path traversal, overflow), boundary values, response structure, config integration checks. 2. Hardened backtest.py: added _DATE_RE regex validation, start>=end guard, moved hardcoded TTL to config, narrowed exception handling (ValueError->422 vs generic->500). 3. Added ttl_backtest to config.py cache section.
analysis: The cycle delivered genuine value: 144 router tests that previously didn't exist, covering input validation, sanitization, and boundary conditions across 8 routers. The backtest router hardening (date validation, config TTL) fixed a real injection vector. However, I have to be honest about two things: (1) My Phase A Finding #1 was wrong — I claimed macro_risk had zero weight but the config already had it wired up; I wasted time investigating a non-bug. (2) The tests are overwhelmingly validation-layer tests (does Pydantic reject bad input?) which are useful but shallow — they test FastAPI/Pydantic behavior more than our code. The deeper value would have been testing actual service integration through the router (mocking DataFetcher, asserting response shapes from the real logic). The BRK.B portfolio analyze test exposed a real numpy warning about empty slices in the portfolio engine, which could be investigated.
what_i_would_do_differently: 1. In Phase A, verify findings against ACTUAL runtime config, not fallback dicts. The macro_risk false positive cost exploration time. 2. Spend less time on Pydantic validation tests (FastAPI already guarantees these) and more time on mocked integration tests — e.g., mock DataFetcher.fetch_market_data to return synthetic data, then assert the full response structure from /api/market-status. That tests OUR code, not Pydantic's. 3. The numpy RuntimeWarning from BRK.B portfolio analyze test suggests portfolio_engine has an edge case with single-holding portfolios with no return history — that's a real bug to chase in the next cycle. 4. Sector router (/api/sectors) was skipped because it has no user input validation, but it should still have response structure tests.
next_steps: Investigate the numpy empty-slice warning in portfolio_engine when analyzing BRK.B (single holding with no yfinance history)
Add mocked integration tests for /api/market-status and /api/sectors (assert response structure with synthetic data)
Investigate the stock_analysis null fields (crash_prob_3m, signal_action) — is this a real bug or lab script artifact?
Add response schema assertions using Pydantic response_model on routers
